description: Negative / minimal case. A short customer-support note that mentions ONE real product issue surrounded by polite small-talk. Tests hallucination resistance — the system must NOT invent stories from social pleasantries, and must produce at most a few well-grounded stories.

deterministic_metrics.name: acceptance_criteria_well_formed
observations: 2/2 stories have AC count in range

reasons.ac_quality: Most acceptance criteria are concrete and testable with clear Given/When/Then structure, though the '95% of synthetic tests over 24-hour period' threshold in ST-01 could specify the denominator more precisely.
reasons.priority_justification: ST-01 is marked High priority but has zero-length rationale (flagged by guardrails), and ST-02's Medium priority also lacks explicit business-impact justification beyond the technical need.
reasons.story_granularity: Both stories represent single, coherent deliverables with clear scope boundaries—ST-01 fixes the DKIM issue, ST-02 adds monitoring—neither is too broad nor atomized into micro-tasks.
reasons.tag_accuracy: Tags are semantically appropriate and mostly consistent ('mobile-app', 'ecommerce' appear in both; 'email' is logical though flagged as non-canonical), showing deliberate vocabulary choices despite minor canonical-set deviations.
reasons.conflict_reasoning: No conflicts are flagged and correctly so—the stories involve email infrastructure work that doesn't intersect with any of the stated constraints around performance thresholds, PCI scope, pharmacy compliance, or integration mandates.

llm_judge.overall_comment: This is a solid, professional backlog synthesis with well-structured stories and testable acceptance criteria that clearly address the reported deliverability problem. The main weakness is missing priority rationales (especially for the High-priority fix), which leaves stakeholders without explicit business justification for sequencing decisions.
